text: The camera view shows the left-hand side of the patient with a blue dot marking the proposed ileostomy site at the upper right of the screen.
The surgeon marks a right angle triangle on the patient's abdomen as a guide for making a Pfannenstiel incision.

The camera view simulates the perspective of a medical student positioned between the patient's legs, holding retractors. The surgeon makes an incision through the various layers, including the Scarpus fascia and the anterior sheath of the rectus, revealing the underlying muscle. The incision measures approximately eight centimeters.
The surgeon opens the incision to gain access to the abdominal cavity, preparing for the hand-assisted ileal pouch anastomosis procedure.
A trocar is placed in the umbilicus, demonstrating the technique used to reduce wound infection rates during the procedure.
A trocar is placed in the right lower quadrant, which will serve as the future ileostomy site. The surgeon utilizes the necessary hole for the ileostomy to insert the trocar.
The surgeon uses a gel port instrument to insufflate the abdominal cavity by injecting air, facilitating access for the laparoscopic procedure.
The surgeon begins working on the right colon, using an instrument to fuse tissue within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon uses a ligature, referred to as the MGI, to achieve excellent hemostasis and facilitate tissue dissection during the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon manipulates the colon on the right side of the abdominal cavity, with the gallbladder and liver visible in the background. The hepatic flexure is being approached, requiring careful navigation to avoid the kidney, ureter, and duodenum.

The duodenum is visible in the distance, clearly marked by the duodenal letters. The colon has been mobilized up to the mid-transverse colon, with the vessels not yet addressed, highlighting a critical step in the procedure.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to preserve the ileocolic artery, a crucial vessel for maintaining pouch length during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the ileocolic artery and then locates the mesentery of the colon distal to it. An incision is made in the mesentery to gain access, initiating the mobilization of the colon, which has not yet been divided.
The surgeon has completed the dissection of the mesentery, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon begins to take the mesentery of the colon at the hepatic flexure, carefully dissecting while monitoring the proximity to the duodenum.
The surgeon ensures the small bowel remains uninjured while completing the mobilization of the colon, preparing to detach it from surrounding structures.

The surgeon has taken the mesentery of the right colon, revealing the small bowel below. The dissection proceeds across the midline, with a visible structure running across the middle at the top of the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament in the anatomic midline of the abdomen and continues dissecting the transverse colon mesentery. The small bowel is visible below, and care is taken to avoid it during the dissection, which progresses towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the splenic flexure, ensuring to avoid injury to the stomach, and continues the dissection down towards the left side of the abdomen.

The surgeon uses manual assistance to detach the terminal ileal mesentery, with the gallbladder visible in the distance, indicating the procedure's location on the right side of the abdomen. This maneuver provides mobility to the right colon segment.
The gel port is removed, and the surgeon mobilizes the colon upward and outward. The terminal ileum is identified, noted for its unique anti-mesenteric fat, which is visible on the small bowel.
The surgeon removes a visible wad of fat and then transects the ileum flush with the ileocecal valve using a stapler, creating a staple line that will become the efferent limb of the J pouch.
The surgeon begins the second stage of the procedure by taking down the ileostomy. The rectum is then removed, and the ileal J pouch is constructed.
The surgeon mobilizes the ileostomy from the anterior abdominal wall, carefully separating it from surrounding adhesions.
The surgeon applies a biofilm to reduce scar tissue formation during the mobilization of the ileostomy. Care is taken to preserve the blood vessels, with slight bleeding visible in the area.
The surgeon carefully lyses adhesions located at the right lower edge, ensuring the small bowel is free to be repositioned into the pelvis for the creation of the ileal J-pouch.

The surgeon uses a stapler to close off the end of the ileum that was previously the ileostomy site, preparing it to become the end of the ileal J-pouch. The incision is visible, with the surgical field oriented between the patient's legs.
The surgeon continues the dissection through the tissue planes, which appear abnormal and bloody, down to the fascia and muscle layers. A wound protector is placed in the incision site.
The surgeon opens the incision down to the muscle layer and places a plastic wound protector to shield the skin from bacteria. The ileostomy is visible at the top left of the screen.

The surgeon uses a wound protector to splay open the incision, enhancing visualization of the pelvic area. An instrument is seen pulling the ileal J pouch down into the pelvis, positioning it to become the apex that will be connected to the anus.

The surgeon assesses the length of two loops of intestine folded back on themselves to ensure the J pouch can reach its intended position. The left side of the screen shows the area where the colon has been removed during the initial surgery.

The surgeon is developing the suprahemorrhoidal artery, a crucial vessel in the procedure. Using a tissue ligator visible on the right, they control hemostasis and enter the presacral space, also known as the total mesorectal plane, which is important for both IBD and rectal cancer management.

The surgeon continues the dissection downwards, revealing a clear surgical plane with a visible layer of fat at the inferior border. A small blood vessel crosses this plane, demonstrating the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the mesorectum and begins dissection within the anatomic plane, which is characterized by minimal blood loss due to its bloodless nature.

The surgeon continues the dissection down to the pelvis, carefully avoiding the prostate in this male patient. The dissection proceeds circumferentially around the rectum until it is fully mobilized down to the pelvic floor, preparing for rectal transection.

The surgeon folds the J pouch back on itself and pulls it down to ensure it reaches the desired location. Stitches are placed at the apex of the J pouch, which will form the anastomosis to the anus. The two limbs of the J pouch are oriented and secured in place.

The surgeon points to the efferent limb of the J pouch, highlighting a stitch placed to prevent twisting. The afferent limb is held up, showing its orientation and potential signs of inflammation consistent with Crohn's disease during pouchoscopy.

The surgeon creates a small enterotomy at the base of the J pouch to gain access, then inserts a large surgical stapler. The stapler is positioned on both sides of the J pouch, causing minor tearing without compromising the structure.

The surgeon uses a 100mm stapler with two lines and a knife in between to fire and seal the J pouch, fusing the two limbs together. The staple line is visible in the middle, and the stapler is reinserted to extend the length of the J pouch.
The completed J-pouch, measuring between fifteen to twenty-five centimeters in length, is shown with a sponge at the bottom to keep it dry.
The surgeon removes the lining using an instrument with sharp hooks, which is placed onto the tissue. The hooks appear sharp and are used to manipulate the lining effectively.

The surgeon uses a Lone Star Retractor to efface the anus, providing access to the anal canal. The hand-sewn ileoanal anastomosis is visible, and the surgeon identifies the dentate line using a Bovie electrocautery device.
The surgeon points to the area and scores it to identify the dentate line, aiming to remove all the mucosal lining as part of the mucosectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs a circumferential dissection by infiltrating saline to raise the mucosa from the underlying smooth muscle, identified as the internal anal sphincter. The mucosa is carefully elevated without injuring the sphincter to preserve its function, and dissection of the mucosa around the area is initiated.

The surgeon uses forceps to pull down the mucosa and begins dissecting it away from the underlying smooth muscle, which appears white. The dissection occurs in the submucosal plane, removing the entire mucosa in a mucosectomy procedure.

The J pouch is visualized from a top view as it is brought down through the remnant rectal cuff. Sutures are placed at four quadrant areas to secure the pouch in position. The edges of the pouch are then manipulated and brought down to complete the anastomosis.

The surgeon stitches the J pouch onto the anal side, incorporating both muscle and mucosa. The posterior side of the J pouch is held by mesentery, highlighting the importance of length in reaching the anus during the procedure.
The surgeon lays down the J pouch and completes the hand-sewn ileoanal anastomosis. The video shows the creation of a diverting loop ileostomy, with the ileostomy visibly being formed.
